items.evidence_text:
Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Advisor: Great. Now, monthly spending—what does it cost to run the household each month, all-in? Mortgage or rent, groceries, utilities, everything.
Client 2: We’re around five thousand a month, give or take.
Client 1: It’s a bit under that—$4,800 a month is the number we track.
Client 2: Okay, fine—$4,800. I round up because it feels safer.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll write $4,800 per month as the baseline expenses. Now debt—do you have any monthly debt payments? Credit cards you carry, car loans, student loans?

Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Client 2: Right—$0. Sorry, I always think “card” equals “debt.”
Advisor: No, that’s super common. Okay, shifting to assets—investable assets first, then we’ll hit the house. Rober, what’s the total you’d say you have across investable accounts—brokerage, retirement, cash—roughly?
Client 1: Roughly $300k. I think it’s $298,750 if you want the exact.
Client 2: I thought it was like $280k? Did it drop?
Client 1: No—Mary, you’re thinking of just the brokerage after we moved some cash. Total investable is $298,750.

Advisor: Most people do. Now the home—do you own your primary residence, and what’s the approximate value you use?
Client 2: Yes, we own. Around two hundred thousand?
Client 1: $199,600 is what we’ve been using.
Advisor: And any mortgage or home equity loan outstanding on it?
Client 1: No, no loan outstanding.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Client 1: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier, Rober, about taxes in the taxable account—we’ll circle there. Before that, I just want to confirm a couple household basics so I don’t assume wrong.
Advisor: You two are married, right—like legally married or civil partners?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Married, yep.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you two doing today?
Client 2: Doing okay—traffic was annoying, but we made it.
Client 1: Yeah, we’re good. Let’s just get into it.
Advisor: Totally. So, tell me a little about what prompted you to come in now, Rober—what are you hoping we nail down?
Client 1: Mainly retirement trajectory and taxes. The taxable account throws off stuff and I want to make sure we’re not being dumb about it.

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you two doing today?
Client 2: Doing okay—traffic was annoying, but we made it.
Client 1: Yeah, we’re good. Let’s just get into it.
Advisor: Totally. So, tell me a little about what prompted you to come in now, Rober—what are you hoping we nail down?

Client 2: Two adults.
Client 1: Just the two of us, yeah.
Advisor: Perfect. And the way I work with you—this is a U.S. RIA setup, meaning advice-first, not a commission brokerage relationship. Does that match what you were expecting coming in?
Client 2: Wait—RIA means you’re like a broker? Or—sorry, I always mix that up.
Client 1: No, it’s not a broker, Mary.

Advisor: Totally fair. So “growth,” with a safety buffer. Now taxes—do you know roughly what bracket you’re in federally? Like 24, 32, 35…
Client 2: I think we’re in the 25% one? Or—sorry, I always mix those up.
Client 1: No, it’s higher. We’re at 35% federal.
Advisor: Got it—35% federal bracket. Mary, when you said 25, you were thinking of an older bracket name—no worries.
Client 2: Okay, thanks. Yeah, that’s what I meant—just “not the top-top,” but still high.

Client 1: It’s a bit under that—$4,800 a month is the number we track.
Client 2: Okay, fine—$4,800. I round up because it feels safer.
Advisor: That makes sense. I’ll write $4,800 per month as the baseline expenses. Now debt—do you have any monthly debt payments? Credit cards you carry, car loans, student loans?
Client 2: We have a card but we pay it off, so… I guess yes?
Client 1: No, we don’t carry a balance. Monthly debt cost is zero.

Client 2: New York. NY.
Client 1: Yep, New York.
Advisor: Great—NY. And when you think about your overall investing comfort level, would you say you’re more conservative, moderate, or growth-oriented?
Client 1: Growth. We can handle swings if it’s diversified.
Client 2: I’m—yeah, growth, but I want the cash buffer to feel solid.

Client 2: Net is like… maybe eight-ish a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross before taxes, like what shows on W-2s. Do you know the annual gross for the household?
Client 1: Yeah, gross household is about $125,350 a year.
Client 2: Wait, is that both of us? I thought mine was separate.
Client 1: It is both—your salary plus mine. It totals to $125,350.

Advisor: Good question—no, gross is before any deductions like 401(k), health insurance, taxes. Take-home is what hits the bank.
Client 2: Okay, got it.
Client 1: My gross is $99,750 a year.
Advisor: Hang on—just writing that. $99,750 gross for you, Rober.
Client 1: Correct.

Client 1: Yeah, tiny. About $1,200 on a retirement platform—private markets.
Client 2: I always forget that exists.
Advisor: Most people do. Now the home—do you own your primary residence, and what’s the approximate value you use?
Client 2: Yes, we own. Around two hundred thousand?
Client 1: $199,600 is what we’ve been using.

Client 1: Yes, I’m employed.
Advisor: And what’s your role day-to-day—like broadly, what field are you in?
Client 1: Finance. I’m in finance—so yeah, numbers all day.
Client 2: Which is why he gets twitchy when I round things.
Client 1: I don’t get twitchy—okay, maybe a little.

Advisor: Cool. Going back to what you said earlier about wanting it systematic—before we talk tactics, I want to make sure I’ve got the income side clean, because that drives everything.
Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: Rober, starting with you—are you currently employed?
Client 1: Yes, I’m employed.
Advisor: And what’s your role day-to-day—like broadly, what field are you in?

Advisor: While we’re on the people details, Rober—can I confirm your date of birth?
Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: I have March 19th, 1983—does that look right?
Client 1: Yes—1983-03-19.
Client 2: Wait, is that like… you need his age or the actual date?

Advisor: Hi Rober, hi Mary—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, how are you two doing today?
Client 2: Doing okay—traffic was annoying, but we made it.
Client 1: Yeah, we’re good. Let’s just get into it.

Client 1: Mainly retirement trajectory and taxes. The taxable account throws off stuff and I want to make sure we’re not being dumb about it.

Advisor: Hang on—just writing that. $99,750 gross for you, Rober.
Client 1: Correct.
Advisor: And Mary, you’ve got income too—earlier we talked about the household being about $125k-ish annualized. Is your piece about $25,600 gross for the year?
Client 2: Yeah, about $25,600.
Client 1: It’s $25,600, yes—don’t say “about,” it’s pretty exact.

Advisor: Got it. And just so I describe it correctly, what would you call your work situation when you were earning that—were you an employee somewhere, or was it more like your own business income?
Client 2: It was my own thing, like I run a small business.
Client 1: Well—she’s not running it right now, but yeah, she’s a business owner.
Advisor: Okay, business owner as the occupation group—thanks. And you’re currently not working at the moment, as you said.
Client 2: Right.
